coredata.dc:title: Single vs sandwich aptamers: Towards the detection of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 using composites of phthalocyanine and nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Centane, Sixolile
Mgidlana, Sithi
Openda, Yolande
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: The superiority of the sandwich over a single aptamer based aptasensor assay for the detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) is demonstrated for the first time. Cobalt tris-3,5 dimethoxy-phenoxy pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhPyCPc) and sulphur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) nanocomposite (SNGQDs@CeO2NPs) were used for electrode modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) both individually and combined to form the substrates: GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, GCE/CoMPhPyCPc and GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc. The designed substrates were used as immobilization platforms for the amino functionalized HB5 aptamer for the development of both single and sandwich aptasensor assays. A novel bioconjugate, made of the HB5 aptamer and nanocomposite (HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs) was fabricated, and characterized using ultra-violet/visible, Fourier transform infrared, and Raman spectroscopies as well as scanning electron microscopy. HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs was applied as a secondary aptamer in the design of novel sandwich assays towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. The performance of the designed aptasensors were evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The sandwich assay gave low limit of detection of 0.00088 pg/mL, high sensitivity of 773925 Ω pg-1mL, showed stability, and good precision in real samples towards HER2 detection.

head.ce:title: Single vs sandwich aptamers: Towards the detection of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 using composites of phthalocyanine and nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The superiority of the sandwich over a single aptamer based aptasensor assay for the detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) is demonstrated for the first time. Cobalt tris-3,5 dimethoxy-phenoxy pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhPyCPc) and sulphur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) nanocomposite (SNGQDs@CeO2NPs) were used for electrode modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) both individually and combined to form the substrates: GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, GCE/CoMPhPyCPc and GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc. The designed substrates were used as immobilization platforms for the amino functionalized HB5 aptamer for the development of both single and sandwich aptasensor assays. A novel bioconjugate, made of the HB5 aptamer and nanocomposite (HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs) was fabricated, and characterized using ultra-violet/visible, Fourier transform infrared, and Raman spectroscopies as well as scanning electron microscopy. HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs was applied as a secondary aptamer in the design of novel sandwich assays towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. The performance of the designed aptasensors were evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The sandwich assay gave low limit of detection of 0.00088 pg/mL, high sensitivity of 773925 Ω pg-1mL, showed stability, and good precision in real samples towards HER2 detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The functionality of an electrochemical biosensor is based on its ability to convert the biochemical events occurring on the electrode surface into electrical signals [1,2]. The electrode surface is modified with biomolecules, for signal amplification and specificity [3–6]. Aptamers are employed in this work for specific detection of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). Aptamers are short single-stranded oligonucleotides that bind to specific targets in the same manner as antibodies, but have various advantages including wide range of targets, high affinity, stability, selectivity, small size, and ease of synthesis [7,8].

In a single aptamer-based sensor, the signal is measured upon direct interaction of an aptamer immobilized on the electrode with the respective antigen [9]. A sandwich based aptasensor, utilizes signal amplification through a double interaction system: a pair of aptamers is used where one is immobilized on the surface to capture the analyte and the second one binds to the captured target for signal generation and enhancement [10–12]. Single aptamer biosensors suffer from weak sensitivity [12]. To overcome the limitation, sandwich type biosensors are employed as an alternative. This paper reports on a comparative study, of an aptamer based on single and sandwich label free electrochemical biosensors towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. There are reports on the use of sandwich antibody assays for electrochemical detection of HER2 [13,14]. As stated above aptamers have many advantages compared to antibodies [7,8,15]. Thus, in this work we employ a sandwich aptamer for the detection of HER2. Sandwich aptamers have been used for the detection of HER2 but using aptamers such as tetrahedral DNA [16,17]. In this work we use HB5 for sandwich aptamer for the first time. The superior qualities of the HB5 compared to other aptamers for the detection of HER2 has been demonstrated [18]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on comparative performance of a single and sandwich aptamer-based assays towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 in the presence of nanoparticles (NPs) and a phthalocyanine (Pc). The NPs used are the composites of sulphur-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) (SNGQDs@CeO2NPs). The composite was synthesized according to the methods reported for growing CeO2 nanoparticles on carbon nanomaterial [19]. All materials were applied for immobilization of the HB5-aptamer biorecognition element as well for signal amplification.

Phthalocyanines are 18 π electron conjugated systems that possess high chemical and photo stability [20]. The phthalocyanine of choice is asymmetrically substituted and contain a redox active cobalt central metal. Compared to the symmetric Pc analogues, the asymmetric Pcs have been reported to have improved electron transfer and redox properties [21]. In addition, carboxylic acid ligand was intentionally chosen for immobilization of the HB5 aptamer via an amide linkage. The sulphur-nitrogen doped graphene quantum (SNGQDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticle (CeO2NPs) composite (SNGQDs@CeO2NPs) were used along with the CoPc for enhanced performance towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. A novel bioconjugate HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs was used as a secondary aptamer for the design of novel sandwich aptasensors for the electrochemical detection of HER2. We have reported on a single aptamer for the detection of HER2 in the presence of cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and sulfur-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and a symmetric CoPc [22]. This work is the first report of the use of sandwich aptamers in the presence of an asymmetric Pc and the NPs for the detection of HER2. The Pc employed is cobalt tris-3,5 dimethoxy-phenoxy, pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhPyCPc). The NPs are expected to enhance the electrocatalytic activity of CoMPhPyCPc towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 in the presence of sandwich aptamers.

The following materials were purchased from Sigma Aldrich: human serum (H6914), cerium (IV) sulphate, N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl, K4[Fe(CN)6], K3[Fe(CN)6], dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 1-pentanol, 1, 8-diazabicyclo (5.4.0) undec-7-ene (DBU), cobalt acetate, and the protein (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2, HER2). 25 % ammonia were purchased from B and M Scientific. The amine-functionalized HB5 DNA aptamer: (5′-/5 AmMC6/AAC CGC CCA AAT CCC TAA GAG TCT GCA CTT GTC ATT TTG TAT ATG TAT TTG GTT TTT GGC TCT CAC AGA CAC ACT ACA CAC GCA CA-3′) was synthesized and purchased from Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT), South Africa. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared using weighed amounts of Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, KCl and NaCl in ultra-pure water from ELGA, Veolia PURELAB, flex system (Marlow, UK). The sulphur – nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) [23], COOH functionalized CeO2NPs (as a separate entity) [24], 4 - (3, 5- dimethoxy-phenoxy) phthalonitrile [25] and pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalonitrile [26] were synthesized as reported in literature.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies were performed using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version 2.1. The EIS experiments were performed between 0.1 and 100000 Hz, using 0.01 VRMS sinusoidal modulation. A non-linear least squares (NLLS) method based on the EQUIVCRT programme was used for automatic fitting of the obtained EIS data. Infrared spectra were recorded on a Bruker® Alpha IR (100 FT-IR) spectrophotometer. Elemental analysis was done using a Vario-Elementar Microcube® ELlII, while mass spectral data were collected on a Bruker® AutoFLEXIII smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as the matrix in the positive ion mode. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman spectral data. The surface of the modified electrodes was assessed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the TESCAN Vega TS 5136LM model. Glassy carbon plates (GCP, Goodfellow, UK) of 1 × 1 cm and 2 mm thick were used as a substrate for SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cobalt tris-3,5 dimethoxy-phenoxy pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhPyCPc), Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: To a reaction flask containing cobalt acetate (44 mg, 0.25 mmol), a stoichiometric mixture (1:3) of 4 - (3, 5- dimethoxy-phenoxy) phthalonitrile and (0.25 g, 0.94 mmol) and pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalonitrile (0.67 g, 2.83 mmol) was added. The contents were then dissolved in 1-pentanol (5 mL), accompanied by dropwise addition of DBU (0.2 mL). The solution was subjected to heating at 160 °C for 9 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting product CoMPhPyCPc was precipitated out using methanol (100 %). The product was further purified using chromatographic methods on a Si60 column with methanol/THF (1: 5 ratio). Yield: 0.42 g (38.2 %). UV/Vis (DMSO): λmax, nm, (log ε), 675 (5.30), 610 (4.97) and 311 (5.55). IR (Platinum ATR): νmax, cm−1, 3067 (carboxylic OH), 1762 (carboxylic C = O), 1594, 1432 (C = C, pyridine C = N). Elemental Analysis for C62O12N9H43Co·2H2O: Calc. C: 62.17, N:10.49 Found. C: 61.26, N: 8.25. MALDI–TOF MS m/z: Calcd.: 1164.24 Found: ([M]) 1164.83.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SNGQDs and CeO2NPs nanocomposite (SNGQDs@CeO2NPs), Scheme 2 (i)
ce:section.ce:para: The CeO2NPs are grown onto the sp2 framework of the SNGQDs as follows: SNGQDs (15 mg) were dissolved in Millipore water (10 mL) followed by sonication to ensure dissolution, Scheme 2(i). In an empty flask (1.0 g, 0.003 mmol) of cerium (IV) sulphate was added, followed by the addition of the SNGQDs solution, the mixture was then immediately subjected to stirring. The brownish yellow mixture, was left to stir for another 30 min at room temperature. To form the nanoparticles, 25 % aqueous ammonia (1.4 M) was added dropwise until precipitation occurred. The resultant mixture was left under continuous stirring for another 2 h. The resulting SNGQDs@CeO2NPs were washed with water and centrifuged to ensure purification. To speed up the drying process, the nanocomposite was further washed with ethanol. The SNGQDs@CeO2NPs were dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs with the HB5 aptamer to form HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate, Scheme 2(ii)
ce:section.ce:para: The SNGQDs@CeO2NPs contain graphene quantum dots and as a result has carboxylic acid functionalized edges. The HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate was formed by amide bond formation between the NH2-HB5 aptamer and carboxylated SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, Scheme 2 (ii). The conjugate was formed as follows: 1 mg of SNGQDs@CeO2NPs was dispersed in PBS (300 µL, pH 7.4). Then, EDC and NHS (1.0 mmol·L− 1 each) were added to activate the –COOH groups followed by shaking (using a shaker) for 4 h. Following this, 100 µL of the HB5 aptamer (0.75 µM) was added to the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs to induce nucleophilic attack onto the activated carbonyl carbons of the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs. The resulting mixture was kept for 2 h under moderate shaking in the dark to allow successful amide bond formation. To remove the non-conjugated SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, the mixture was centrifuged (5000 rpm for 20 min) and subsequently washed with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The precipitate was suspended in PBS (100 µL, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C. The synthesized HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate is ready for modification of electrode.

All the solutions were freshly prepared in Milli-Q water and stored at the temperature of 4 °C until use. Solutions were deaerated by bubbling with argon gas prior to the electrochemistry experiments and the electrochemical cell was kept under argon atmosphere throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the modified glassy carbon electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Glassy carbon electrodes were first polished with Al2O3 powder (Aldrich, 0.1, 0.05 mm) using Metrohm polishing kit, and rinsed with deionized water, followed by sonication in ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the electrodes were allowed to dry at room temperature. On the newly cleaned GCE surfaces, the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite alone, CoMPhPyCPc alone and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc (sequential adsorption) were added to form substrates: GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs (1), GCE/CoMPhPyCPc (2) and GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc (3) respectively. The numbers in brackets are electrode numbers and will be used throughout the manuscript.

For electrode modification purposes the amounts of the modifies employed are: SNQGDs@CeO2NPs (10 µL, 1 mg/mL, water) and CoMPhPyCPc (10 µL, 8.57 × 10-4 mol in DMF). The modifiers were dropped onto the GCE accordingly, and left in the oven to dry. In the case of the GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc (3) surface, the SNQGDs@CeO2NPs (10 µL, 1 mg/mL in water) was added onto the electrode first forming GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, followed by addition of 10 µL of the CoMPhPyCPc (10 µL, 8.57 × 10-4 mmol), forming GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc (3). We have shown that the best catalytic activity is obtained when MPcs are placed on top of nanomaterials [27], hence in this work for the sequentially modified electrodes, CoMPhPyCPc was placed on top.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of HB5 aptamer onto modified GCE, Scheme 2(iii)
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilization of the amino functionalized HB5 aptamer on the electrode was achieved by means of an amide bond formation, with the carboxylated CoMPhPyCPc and SNQGDs@CeO2NPs via DCC/NHS coupling chemistry. To the modified electrodes, GCE/(1), GCE/(2) and GCE/(3): DCC (6.0 mg, 0.029 mmol) and NHS (4.0 mg, 0.035 mmol) were added, the electrodes were left at room temperature for 1 h to activate the carboxylic groups for nucleophilic substitution by the NH2-HB5 aptamer. To the activated surfaces, 10 µL of the HB5 aptamer (0.75 µM) was added to form aptasensors: GCE/(1)/HB5, GCE/(2)/HB5 and GCE/(3)/HB5, respectively. The resulting electrodes were further rinsed with PBS to remove any unbound HB5 on the electrode surface.

The three aptasensors: GCE/(1)/HB5, GCE/(2)/HB5 and GCE/(3)/HB5 were used as base for single aptamer and sandwich assays. First for the single aptamer, all the above electrodes were treated with 1% BSA to prevent non-specific binding, Scheme 2(iii). The electrochemical response of the electrodes in the presence of varying concentrations of the HER2 antigen (0 pg – 21 pg) was measured using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS measurements were conducted under the stable open circuit potential with the frequency from 0.1 to 100000 Hz, using 0.01 VRMS sinusoidal modulation. The resulting electrodes are represented as: GCE/(1)/HB5/HER2, GCE/(2)/HB5/HER2 and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 and were incubated at 37° C for 60 min. The electrodes were then rinsed with PBS buffer to remove any unbound HER2 species on the electrode. For the sandwich assay, the (10 µL, 0.11 µg/mL) HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate was added to GCE/(1)/HB5/HER2, GCE/(2)/HB5/HER2 and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 followed by incubation at 37° C for another 60 min to form the aptamer-HER2-aptamer sandwich assay for all electrodes, Scheme 2(iii). The electrodes are represented as GCE/(1)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, GCE/(2)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs.

Preliminary experiments for the determination of HER2 protein in human serum samples were also performed. The tests were done both in single and sandwich aptamer assays. The male human serum albumin was stored at − 20 °C. Serum samples (diluted 1:500 in PBS) were spiked with HER2 at different concentrations (1 pg/mL, 3 pg/mL, 5 pg/mL and 7 pg/mL) and analysed without further treatments. The response of the aptasensors was then determined by EIS measurements, under the same conditions used for HER2 calibration curve.

The repeatability of the designed probes was investigated by running successive measurements at each HER2 concentrations in triplicate for each aptasensor, and the standard deviation was calculated. The stability of the designed sensors was assessed in terms of storage and/or shelf life time over 96 h at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the cobalt tris-3,5 dimethoxy-phenoxy pyridine (5) oxy (2)- carboxylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhPyCPc)
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis of the asymmetrical CoMPhPyCPc. The complex was synthesized using two dissimilar phthalonitriles in the presence of 1-pentanol, DBU and cobalt acetate. DBU is used as a catalyst following literature methods, for the synthesis of phthalocyanines [28]. A ratio of 1:3 of the phthalonitriles (for the formation of AB3 compounds with B being the dominant substituent) was chosen as this ratio is often used if the reactivities of two phthalonitrile involved in the cross condensation are similar [28]. This ratio leads to the formation of symmetric A4 compound (33%), the target asymmetric AB3 phthalocyanine as a major product (44%), and the remaining A2B2, A3B, and B4 compounds as minor products (23%) [28]. The yield of 38% obtained shows that this is the correct ratio.

The synthesis of the compound was confirmed by; UV/Vis, FTIR and mass spectrometer, and elemental analysis. The elemental analysis results are in agreement with observations that some Pcs are isolated as hydrates [29]. 1H NMR spectra were not recorded due to the paramagnetic nature of the central metal. The mass spectrum is shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). The phthalocyanine of interest showed a Q-band and Soret band at wavelengths 675 nm and 311 nm, respectively, Fig. 1 A. The Q band is broad suggesting aggregation in DMSO.

The nanocomposite was fabricated by in-situ synthesis of cerium oxide nanoparticles on the graphene quantum dots surface, following literature methods [19]. Several characterization techniques were applied to confirm the successful synthesis of the composite.

Fig. 1B shows the UV–Vis data of the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite, plotted against that of the SNGQDs and COOH functionalized CeO2NPs for comparison sake. Please note that individual COOH-CeO2NPs have been shown [24] to be of rod like shape, Fig. S2.

An absorption peak of SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite was observed at 349 nm, which is around the region where both the SNGQDs and CeO2NPs absorb [30,31], Fig. 1B. The chemical composition of the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs nanocomposite was further characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. S3 (Supporting Information) shows the FTIR spectra of SNGQDs, CeO2NPs and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs. The comparison is necessary to confirm the structural changes if any, during formation of the composite. The SNGQDs contain carboxylated edges as evidenced by the C–C, C = O/C = C and –OH at 1381 cm−1, 1558 cm−1 and 3281 cm−1, respectively. Similarly, the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs had a peak at 3368 cm−1 can be attributed to the –OH groups present at the SNGQDs edges within the composite, slightly shifted compared to SNGQDs alone at 3281 cm−1. The peaks at 1749 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 for SNGQDs@CeO2NPs can be attributed to the C = O/C = C carbons from the carboxylic edges as well as the sp2 hybridized graphene carbon framework, respectively. The shift to higher wavenumbers might suggests the weakening of aromatic ring stacking due to the cerium nanoparticles being intercalated into the SNGQDs sheets [32].

The peaks at 1419 cm−1 , 1065 cm−1 and 997 cm−1 can be attributed to –OH bending, C-O stretch and C–H bending all from the SNGQDs in SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite, Fig. S3. The successful synthesis of the CeO2NPs on the SNGQDs surface, can be confirmed by the 488 cm−1 and 546 cm−1 peaks which can be attributed to the O-Ce-O stretching mode of vibration. The IR data suggests the successful synthesis of the CeO2NPs on the SNQGQDs surface, while preserving the structural integrity of the SNGQDs framework. Raman spectroscopy was used to further confirm the synthesis of the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite, Fig. 2 . The Raman spectroscopy of SNGQDs is characterized by the D-band (1297 cm−1) and G-bands (1597 cm−1) which correspond to impurity defects on the carbon structure and the in-plane symmetry on the sp2 orbital of the carbon, respectively [33]. The Raman spectrum of the CeO2NPs on the other hand, had the fundamental Raman active mode at 464 cm−1, which is the triply degenerate F2g mode that corresponds to a symmetric Ce-O stretching vibration in the octahedral point group [34]. Additional bands: 595 cm−1 due to replacement of cerium (IV) atoms by cerium (III) atoms, 823 cm−1 and 895 cm−1 due O-O stretching vibration of peroxide and lastly 989 cm−1 and 1051 cm−1 due to second order scattering with former emanating from the combination of the A1g, Eg and F2g modes [35,36]. The Raman data obtained for the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs exhibited characteristic Raman modes of both the SNGQDs and CeO2NPs. The first three peaks are of CeO2NPs character, whilst the last two are of the SNGQDs character within the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite. These results further confirm the successful synthesis of CeO2NPs on the SNGQDs surface, to form SNGQDs@CeO2NPs.

The bioconjugate was formed via amide linkage of the HB5 aptamer and the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs composite through EDC and NHS coupling. Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR data of the HB5 aptamer alone (a) HB5 aptamer and the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate (b). The HB5-aptamer (Fig. 3(a)) shows various common functional groups C-N, C-O, C = O and most importantly –NH2 stretch, as demonstrated by the two fangs around the 3409–3221 cm−1 region. The functional groups present in the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs have been discussed above. The successful conjugation of the HB5 aptamer to the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs nanocomposite is confirmed by the IR spectrum obtained for the resulting bioconjugate HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, Fig. 3(b). The bioconjugate spectra, had the same functional groups found in both the HB5 aptamer and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs. The amide bond formation was confirmed by the collapse of the previously observed two fangs indicative of the presence of the –NH2 in the HB5 aptamer sample, to now a single peak at 3267 cm−1 for the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs sample implying successful conjugation via amide bond formation.

The CV technique was used to evaluate the electrode modification in 1 mM ferro/ferricyanide in 0.01 M KCl in 10 mM PBS, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The CVs for the ferro/ferricyanide system help in understanding electron transfer at the interface of the modified electrodes. Fig. 4A shows the CV obtained for the bare GCE (insert), GCE/(1), GCE/(2), and GCE/(3) surfaces. The bare GCE showed a reversible peak pair, with oxidation and reduction peak separation (ΔEp) = 68 mV, Table S1 (Supporting Information). The GCE/(1) showed two oxidation peaks. The peaks labelled I in all CVs in Fig. 4A are due to the ferro/ferricyanide. The peak labelled II for GCE/1 could be due to SNGQDs, Fig. 4A, which are known to have a peak in this region [37]. This peak could also be observed for SNGQDs@CeO2NPs alone in buffer (without Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) though weaker in Fig. 4B. The peak labelled II in GCE/2 (CoMPhPyCPc alone) alone is due to Co(III)/Co(II) redox process according to literature [38]. This peak could also be observed for CoMPhPyCPc alone in buffer in Fig. 4B. This peak is also observed in GCE/3 (which combines both CoMPhPyCPc and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs). The additional broad peak observed near 0.3 V for GCE/3 could be due to CeO2 which is known to show CV peaks in this region [39]. The modification of the bare GCE, with the various nanomaterials to form the electrodes (1–3) resulted in increased redox peak separation (ΔEp). The GCE/(3) showed a smaller peak separation (110 mV) compared to that of GCE/(2) (120 mV), Table S1, accompanied by an increased oxidation peak currents. This further demonstrates the synergistic effect of combining a particular CoMPhPyCPc with SNGQDs@CeO2NPs for enhanced electron transfer in sensor design.

The real surface area (A) was calculated using Randles Sevcik equation (1) [40] and using Fig. 4A (peaks I, for Fe(CN)6]3-/4-)

where Ip , n, A, C, D and v are the peak current, the number of electrons involved, the surface area, the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at 25 °C, and the scan rate, respectively. The literature value for K3[Fe(CN)6] D = 7.6 × 10-6 cm2 s−1 [41] and n = 1, were employed.

The calculated effective area was used to calculate surface coverage using equation (2) [42].

where Γ is the surface coverage, Q is the charge (from Fig. 4B), n the number of electrons transferred, F the Faraday’s constant (96,485C.mol−1) and A is the effective surface area calculated from Eq. (1). The SNGQDs@AuNPs modified electrode, GCE/1 and the CoMPhPyCPc based GCE/2 had a surface coverage of 2.45 × 10-8 mol.cm −2 and 5.94 × 10-10 mol.cm −2 respectively, Table S1. Upon combination of the SNGQDs@AuNPs and CoMPhPyCPc on the electrode to form the surface GCE/3, a surface coverage of 2.89 × 10-9 mol.cm −2, hence the surface coverage is higher for the composite compared to the phthalocyanine alone. This result further demonstrates the significance of combining MPcs with various nanomaterials for enhanced electrocatalytic behaviour.

EIS is well known as an effective tool for studying the properties of modified electrodes [43]. A typical impedance spectrum, presented in the form of the Nyquist plot, includes a semicircle portion at higher frequencies corresponding to the electron transfer limited process and a linear part at lower frequency range representing diffusion limited process. EIS measurements were carried out for the various electrodes (1–3), and the Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 4C. The data obtained were fitted using a modified equivalent circuit. The key parameter used to differentiate and characterize the modified electrodes is the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which increases with decrease in conductivity and vice versa. The obtained data for the EIS data were in agreement with that obtained in cyclic voltammetry: the surface with the lowest ΔEp had the lowest Rct values, Table S1.

The SEM analysis of the modified surface GCE/1, GCE/2 and GCE/3 was performed, the obtained results are shown in Fig. S4. A dispersed distribution of the SNGQDs@AuNPs is observed for GCE/1. The CoMPhPyCPc based GCE/2 shows a grey surface, with little to no visibility of particles; this is due to the small MPc size ∼ 1 nm. When the SNGQDs@AuNPs and CoMPhPyCPc were added to the GCE (SNGQDs@AuNPs first), to form GCE/3 a uniform grey layer was formed on the electrode. There GCE surface for GCE/3 is well covered with no bare spots.

The biorecognition of choice in this work is the HB5 aptamer. The used aptamer concentration (0.75 µM) is based on our previously reported optimization data [24]. This concentration was used for all modified surfaces and the resulting electrodes are represented as: GCE/(1)/HB5, GCE/(2)/HB5 and GCE/(3)/HB5. Higher Rct values were obtained for all substrates (electrodes 1–3), upon introduction of the HB5 aptamer on all surfaces, Fig. 4D. This might be due to the fact that the aptamer is an oligonucleotideand is not redox active, as a result might have partly blocked the electron/ion transport between the electrode and the electrolyte.

The individual aptasensors were then tested towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 in the concentration range 0–––21 pg/mL using EIS. The data is listed in Table S2 (Supporting Information). Fig. 5 shows the obtained EIS data for the single aptamer designed aptasensors, using the best performing electrode GCE/(3)/HB5 as an example (the data for GCE/(1)/HB5 and GCE/(2)/HB5) are provided in Fig. S5 supplementary information). The HB5-HER2 interaction was determined by using an increase in Rct values for [FeCN6]3-/4-.

The figures show that there was an increase in Rct with increase in HER2 concentration. This trend held true for all designed aptasensors. Regression plots were then created using the data of each electrode excluding outliers. The equations (3) of the regression plots were as follows:

The slopes (m) of the regression plots give the sensitivity of each sensor. The sensitivity of a biosensor is defined as the relationship between the change in analyte concentration and the intensity of the signal generated from the transducer [44]. The phthalocyanine alone based GCE/(2)/HB5 aptasensor had a higher sensitivity, compared to the nanocomposite based GCE/(1)/HB5 aptasensor, Table 1 . These results demonstrate the superiority of the CoMPhPyCPc, compared to the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs nanocomposite as an electrode modifier in the design of the HER2 specific aptasensor. This is by means of its superior ability to act as an immobilization platform for the biorecognition element (HB5) to allow for development of a stable aptasensor, while aiding in enhanced electron transfer. The highest sensitivity was obtained when CoMPhPyCPc and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs were combined (in GCE/(3)/HB5) for electrode modification, Table 1, with a sensitivity of 596,285 Ωng-1mL, corresponding to the high surface area compared to Pcs alone. This further demonstrates the significance of combining two or more nanomaterials for enhanced sensing abilities.

The limit of detection (LOD) values for the three single aptasensors were estimated by the equation 3σ/s. In which σ is the standard deviation of Rct values obtained for PBS (without HER2) for all probes, and s is the slope of the regression plots. The lowest LOD was obtained for GCE/(3)/HB5, 0.0059 pg/mL, Table 1. The obtained limit of detection (LOD) value for GCE/(3)/HB5 which is a combination SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and CoMPhPyCPc was much lower than that listed in Table 1 [45–49] for single aptamer assays, showing the importance of combining phthalocyanines with nanoparticles. The LOD reported in this work for a single aptamer using an asymmetrically substituted CoPc is much lower than when symmetric CoPc were employed with values ranging from 0.051 and 0.61 ng/mL [24], showing the importance of symmetry. Various contributors to the aptasensor performance can be proposed. First, the electron transfer properties and great conductivity of the used CoMPhPyCPc and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs alone and when combined. Secondly, the applicability of these nanomaterials as electron modifiers to form a fairly stable modified surface for the immobilization of the biorecognition element (HB5- aptamer) for enhanced specificity and sensitivity.

The sandwich assay is built similar to the single aptamer assay, with an additional step where the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs is added onto the electrode surfaces to complete the sandwich, Scheme 2 (iii). The effect of incubation time of all designed aptasensors with the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate to form the sandwich assay was investigated, Fig. 6 A (using GCE/CoMPhPyCPc (2)/HER2 as an example). GCE/CoMPhPyCPc (2)/HER2 (at [HER2] = 1 pg/mL) was incubated with the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs solution for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 min at 37 °C. Fig. 6A shows that there was an increase in Rct value with increasing incubation time. This held true until 60 min, where an equilibrium was reached. As such, the optimum incubation time with the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate to complete the sandwich assay was 60 min.

The effect of concentration of the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate used was also investigated, Fig. 6B. To GCE/(2)/HER2 (where [HER2] = 5 pg/mL), various concentrations of HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs were added, and the effect of concentration was measured in terms of Rct values. Different concentrations of HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, in the range 0.038 – 0.19 µM, were used to establish a sandwich structure. The Rct values increased with increasing the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs concentration, up until 0.11 µM there after a subsequent decrease was observed at higher concentrations. Thus, 0.11 µM was selected as the optimum concentration for the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate/secondary aptamer to complete the sandwich.

A general increase in the Rct value was observed on addition of HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs to form HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, Table S2. This is attribute to the nature of both the HB5 aptamer and the HER2 antigen which are large protein like structures, as a result impede the electron transfer of the electrode.

The performance of the designed sandwich assays towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 the range 0 – 21 pg/mL were recorded using EIS. Fig. 7 shows the obtained EIS data for GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs (A and B) as an example. The figure clearly show that there was an increase in Rct with increase in HER2 concentration. This trend held true for all designed aptasensors, the data for GCE/(1)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and GCE/(2)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs are shown in Fig. S5 (supporting information) and Table S2. Regression plots were then created using the data of each electrode excluding outliers. Equations (4) of the regression plots were as follows:

GCE/(1)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs:
y = 56485 x + 10534 ,
R
2
= 0.9489
(4a).

The sensitivity of each assay can be deduced from the regression plots, where the slope = sensitivity. The highest sensitivity was obtained for the conjugate-based sandwich assay GCE/3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, equation (4c), Table 1. The major contributing factor is the synergistic effect of CoMPhPyCPc and SNGQDs@CeO2NPs for electron transfer and signal amplification when combined as electrode modifiers towards biosensor design. As evidenced by the data, the sandwich assays showed higher sensitivity compared to single aptamer assays, Table 1. An advantage of the sandwich assay, compared to the single aptamer assay, is the two biorecognition system, one on the electrode surface and the other within the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate. Furthermore, the bioconjugate contains the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs made of SNGQDs and CeO2NPs, which have been previously reported for excellent electrocatalytic activity towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 [22].

The limit of detection (LOD) values were calculated for the sandwich assay, as was done for the single aptamer assay using the sigma (3σ) method as stated in Section 3.4.1. Agreeable to the obtained sensitivities, the lowest LOD was obtained GCE/ (3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs at 0.00088 pg/mL. The LOD values for the rest of the aptasensors are listed in Table 1 together with literature values [16,17]. All the values reported in this work are better than those reported in literature, this shows the advantage of the nanoparticles and the Pc employed in this work, and their combination for sandwich aptasensor. This also shows the superiority of HB5 as an aptamer.

The accuracy of the method was tested, by spiking serum samples from normal human with different concentrations of HER2. The serum was diluted 500-fold. This was to evaluate the ability of the probes to detect or recover HER2, in the competitive human serum environment. The specificity of the various probes was investigated in terms of percentage recoveries at four concentrations: 1 pg/mL, 3 pg/mL, 5 pg/mL and 7 pg/mL. The results obtained for the single and sandwich aptamer assay are shown in Table 2 and Table S3 (in Supporting Information). The Rct data for human serum alone is also shown for GCE/(2) in Table S3. Both the sandwich assay and single aptamer assay, showed recoveries higher than the 100 % maximum acceptable value at 1 pg/mL. The highest %recoveries were obtained for the GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs assay (88–96%, excluding 1 pg/mL). Percentage recoveries outside the acceptable range of 80 – 110% can be attributed to the matrix effect during detection [50]. The results obtained show the feasibility and potential of the aptasensors for real life applications of the detection of HER2 in early breast cancer diagnosis in human serum.

The repeatability of the designed aptasensors is demonstrated throughout, the experiments were run (n = 3) at all concentrations for each aptasensor electrode, Table S2. For each aptamer modified electrode, HER2 concentration ranged from 1 to 21 pg/mL. Experiments were run in triplicate at each HER2 concentration, and the % RSD determined for each [HER2] for n = 3. This is done for all aptasensors where [HER2] detection is concerned. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for all electrodes at various concentrations was determined, acceptable values for biosensor designed were obtained.

Stability is one of the important parameters for the practical application of any sensor, and so it was evaluated. The stabilities of the best performing probes: GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs were determined. The shelf life of each was estimated at [HER2] = 5 ng/mL in 10 Mm PBS (pH 7.4). The probes were stored at 4 °C over a period of 96 h. After every 24-h period, the stability of the sensors was investigated by using Rct values in 1 mM Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4), Fig. 8 . Each measurement was made in triplicate daily for each probe, up until the fourth day. The results showed that the EIS response decreased to about 95% and 90 % for the aptasensors, GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 respectively, of their initial value, indicating a very desirable stability. The sandwich assay showed better stability compared to the single aptamer assay.

Surface regeneration in biosensors can be defined as the ability to regenerate a sensing surface to allow for reusability. The surface regeneration of the best performing probes: GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 was investigated using the chemical method [51]. pH = 2.0 and 7.4 PBS buffers were employed for dissociation and regeneration, respectively. The probes were subjected to a pH 2 PBS buffer for a total of 15 min, to achieve the HER2-HB5 dissociation on the GCE. The electrodes were then rinsed with pH 7.4 PBS buffer to restore the conformation and integrity of the capture probes where possible, prior to proceeding to the next cycle of measurement. Results obtained for both probes are shown in Fig. 9 . The aptasensor GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 showed good surface regeneration, as demonstrated by the progression of dissociation Fig. 9A (a-c). The resulting surface after 15 min, had a comparable Rct value (GCE/(3)/HB5regenerated = 22900 Ω) compared to the original GCE/(3)/HB5 (20400 Ω) prior to surface regeneration experiments, Fig. 9A (d). Similarly, for the probe GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs, Fig. 9B (a-c): At the end of 15 min Rct values of 54900 Ω for GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 regenerated, which was similar to the 55733 Ω Rct value obtained for GCE/(3)/HB5/HER2 prior to adding the HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs bioconjugate to form the sandwich assay. Based on the obtained Rct values obtained post acid treatment, the surfaces based on the GCE/(3) surface were regenerated. The single aptamer based GCE/(3)/HB5regenerated aptasensor was investigated for reusability, for the detection of [HER2] at 5 pg/mL. The Rct value obtained for the new surface GCE/(3)/HB5regenerated/HER2 (57200 Ω) was similar to that obtained originally for GCE/(3)/HB5original/HER2 (55733 Ω). The results further demonstrate the structural flexibility and agility of aptamers as capture probes, where surface regeneration, reusability and cost effectiveness are concerned.

Novel single and sandwich aptamer assays based on the substrates: GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs(1), GCE/CoMPhPyCPc(2) and GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc(3) were developed for the accurate and sensitive detection of HER2. The designed aptasensors, showed a linear response for the impedimetric detection of HER2 in the concentration range of 0 – 21 pg/mL, with the lowest detection limit of 0.00088 pg/mL obtained for the GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc(3)/HB5/HER2/HB5-SNGQDs@CeO2NPs. Thus, the best sensing performance was observed for the GCE/SNGQDs@CeO2NPs/CoMPhPyCPc (3) based aptasensors for both the single aptamer and sandwich assays in terms of LOD and sensitivity. This is due to the enhanced charge transport property and loading capacity of the biomolecules when the SNGQDs@CeO2NPs and CoMPhPyCPc are combined on the GCE for modification. The sandwich assays showed better sensing performance compared to the single aptamer based aptasensors. This can be attributed to the dual aptameric strategy involved in sandwich aptasensor design. The advantage of the single aptamer assay against the sandwich aptamer assay is the time of fabrication, where less time was needed for the former. The real-life applicability of the designed aptasensors was tested in human serum samples. The aptasensors showed great storage stability, sensitivity and specificity towards HER2, implying great potential for applications in early diagnosis of breast cancer.
